Package [org.apache.flink.table.api](<package-summary.html>)

# Interface TableEnvironment

All Known Subinterfaces:
    `[StreamTableEnvironment](<bridge/java/StreamTableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api.bridge.java")`, `[TableEnvironmentInternal](<internal/TableEnvironmentInternal.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api.internal")`

All Known Implementing Classes:
    `[AbstractStreamTableEnvironmentImpl](<bridge/internal/AbstractStreamTableEnvironmentImpl.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api.bridge.internal")`, `[StreamTableEnvironmentImpl](<bridge/java/internal/StreamTableEnvironmentImpl.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api.bridge.java.internal")`, `[TableEnvironmentImpl](<internal/TableEnvironmentImpl.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api.internal")`

* * *

[@PublicEvolving](<../../annotation/PublicEvolving.html> "annotation interface in org.apache.flink.annotation") public interface TableEnvironment

A table environment is the base class, entry point, and central context for creating Table and SQL API programs.

It is unified both on a language level for all JVM-based languages (i.e. there is no distinction between Scala and Java API) and for bounded and unbounded data processing.

A table environment is responsible for:

  * Connecting to external systems.
  * Registering and retrieving [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api")s and other meta objects from a catalog.
  * Executing SQL statements.
  * Offering further configuration options.

The syntax for path in methods such as `createTemporaryView(String, Table)` is following `[[catalog-name.]database-name.]object-name`, where the catalog name and database are optional. For path resolution see `useCatalog(String)` and `useDatabase(String)`.

Example: ``cat.1`.`db`.`Table`` resolves to an object named 'Table' in a catalog named 'cat.1' and database named 'db'.

Note: This environment is meant for pure table programs. If you would like to convert from or to other Flink APIs, it might be necessary to use one of the available language-specific table environments in the corresponding bridging modules.

  * ## Method Details

    * ### create

static [TableEnvironment](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") create([EnvironmentSettings](<EnvironmentSettings.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") settings)

Creates a table environment that is the entry point and central context for creating Table and SQL API programs.

It is unified both on a language level for all JVM-based languages (i.e. there is no distinction between Scala and Java API) and for bounded and unbounded data processing.

A table environment is responsible for:

      * Connecting to external systems.
      * Registering and retrieving [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api")s and other meta objects from a catalog.
      * Executing SQL statements.
      * Offering further configuration options.

Note: This environment is meant for pure table programs. If you would like to convert from or to other Flink APIs, it might be necessary to use one of the available language-specific table environments in the corresponding bridging modules.

Parameters:
    `settings` \- The environment settings used to instantiate the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").
    * ### create

static [TableEnvironment](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") create([Configuration](<../../configuration/Configuration.html> "class in org.apache.flink.configuration") configuration)

Creates a table environment that is the entry point and central context for creating Table and SQL API programs.

It is unified both on a language level for all JVM-based languages (i.e. there is no distinction between Scala and Java API) and for bounded and unbounded data processing.

A table environment is responsible for:

      * Connecting to external systems.
      * Registering and retrieving [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api")s and other meta objects from a catalog.
      * Executing SQL statements.
      * Offering further configuration options.

Note: This environment is meant for pure table programs. If you would like to convert from or to other Flink APIs, it might be necessary to use one of the available language-specific table environments in the corresponding bridging modules.

Parameters:
    `configuration` \- The specified options are used to instantiate the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").
    * ### fromValues

default [Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromValues([Object](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Object.html> "class or interface in java.lang")... values)

Creates a Table from given values.

Examples:

You can use a `row(...)` expression to create a composite rows:

          tEnv.fromValues(
               row(1, "ABC"),
               row(2L, "ABCDE")
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- f0: BIGINT NOT NULL     // original types INT and BIGINT are generalized to BIGINT
           |-- f1: VARCHAR(5) NOT NULL // original types CHAR(3) and CHAR(5) are generalized to VARCHAR(5)
                                       // it uses VARCHAR instead of CHAR so that no padding is applied


The method will derive the types automatically from the input expressions. If types at a certain position differ, the method will try to find a common super type for all types. If a common super type does not exist, an exception will be thrown. If you want to specify the requested type explicitly see `fromValues(AbstractDataType, Object...)`.

It is also possible to use [`Row`](<../../types/Row.html> "class in org.apache.flink.types") object instead of ` row` expressions.

ROWs that are a result of e.g. a function call are not flattened

          public class RowFunction extends ScalarFunction {
               {@literal @}DataTypeHint("ROW<f0 BIGINT, f1 VARCHAR(5)>")
               Row eval();
           }

           tEnv.fromValues(
               call(new RowFunction()),
               call(new RowFunction())
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- f0: ROW<`f0` BIGINT, `f1` VARCHAR(5)>


The row constructor can be dropped to create a table with a single column:

ROWs that are a result of e.g. a function call are not flattened

          tEnv.fromValues(
               1,
               2L,
               3
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- f0: BIGINT NOT NULL


Parameters:
    `values` \- Expressions for constructing rows of the VALUES table.
    * ### fromValues

default [Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromValues([AbstractDataType](<../types/AbstractDataType.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.types")<?> rowType, [Object](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Object.html> "class or interface in java.lang")... values)

Creates a Table from given collection of objects with a given row type.

The difference between this method and `fromValues(Object...)` is that the schema can be manually adjusted. It might be helpful for assigning more generic types like e.g. DECIMAL or naming the columns.

Examples:

          tEnv.fromValues(
               DataTypes.ROW(
                   DataTypes.FIELD("id", DataTypes.DECIMAL(10, 2)),
                   DataTypes.FIELD("name", DataTypes.STRING())
               ),
               row(1, "ABC"),
               row(2L, "ABCDE")
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- id: DECIMAL(10, 2)
           |-- name: STRING


For more examples see `fromValues(Object...)`.

Parameters:
    `rowType` \- Expected row type for the values.
    `values` \- Expressions for constructing rows of the VALUES table.
See Also:

      * `fromValues(Object...)`
    * ### fromValues

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromValues([Expression](<../expressions/Expression.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.expressions")... values)

Creates a Table from given values.

Examples:

You can use a `row(...)` expression to create a composite rows:

          tEnv.fromValues(
               row(1, "ABC"),
               row(2L, "ABCDE")
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
            |-- f0: BIGINT NOT NULL     // original types INT and BIGINT are generalized to BIGINT
            |-- f1: VARCHAR(5) NOT NULL // original types CHAR(3) and CHAR(5) are generalized to VARCHAR(5)
           	 *                          // it uses VARCHAR instead of CHAR so that no padding is applied


The method will derive the types automatically from the input expressions. If types at a certain position differ, the method will try to find a common super type for all types. If a common super type does not exist, an exception will be thrown. If you want to specify the requested type explicitly see `fromValues(AbstractDataType, Expression...)`.

It is also possible to use [`Row`](<../../types/Row.html> "class in org.apache.flink.types") object instead of ` row` expressions.

ROWs that are a result of e.g. a function call are not flattened

          public class RowFunction extends ScalarFunction {
               {@literal @}DataTypeHint("ROW<f0 BIGINT, f1 VARCHAR(5)>")
               Row eval();
           }

           tEnv.fromValues(
               call(new RowFunction()),
               call(new RowFunction())
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- f0: ROW<`f0` BIGINT, `f1` VARCHAR(5)>


The row constructor can be dropped to create a table with a single column:

ROWs that are a result of e.g. a function call are not flattened

          tEnv.fromValues(
               lit(1).plus(2),
               lit(2L),
               lit(3)
           )


will produce a Table with a schema as follows:

          root
           |-- f0: BIGINT NOT NULL


Parameters:
    `values` \- Expressions for constructing rows of the VALUES table.
    * ### fromValues

    * ### registerCatalog

[@Deprecated](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Deprecated.html> "class or interface in java.lang") void registerCatalog([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") catalogName, [Catalog](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") catalog)

Deprecated.

Use `createCatalog(String, CatalogDescriptor)` instead. The new method uses a [`CatalogDescriptor`](<../catalog/CatalogDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") to initialize the catalog instance and store the [`CatalogDescriptor`](<../catalog/CatalogDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") to the [`CatalogStore`](<../catalog/CatalogStore.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog").

Registers a [`Catalog`](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") under a unique name. All tables registered in the [`Catalog`](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") can be accessed.

Parameters:
    `catalogName` \- The name under which the catalog will be registered.
    `catalog` \- The catalog to register.
    * ### createCatalog

void createCatalog([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") catalogName, [CatalogDescriptor](<../catalog/CatalogDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") catalogDescriptor)

Creates a [`Catalog`](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") using the provided [`CatalogDescriptor`](<../catalog/CatalogDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.catalog"). All table registered in the [`Catalog`](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") can be accessed. The [`CatalogDescriptor`](<../catalog/CatalogDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") will be persisted into the [`CatalogStore`](<../catalog/CatalogStore.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog").

Parameters:
    `catalogName` \- The name under which the catalog will be created
    `catalogDescriptor` \- The catalog descriptor for creating catalog

    * ### registerFunction

[@Deprecated](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Deprecated.html> "class or interface in java.lang") void registerFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") name, [ScalarFunction](<../functions/ScalarFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") function)

Deprecated.

Use `createTemporarySystemFunction(String, UserDefinedFunction)` instead. Please note that the new method also uses the new type system and reflective extraction logic. It might be necessary to update the function implementation as well. See the documentation of [`ScalarFunction`](<../functions/ScalarFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") for more information on the new function design.

Registers a [`ScalarFunction`](<../functions/ScalarFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") under a unique name. Replaces already existing user-defined functions under this name.

    * ### createTemporarySystemFunction

void createTemporarySystemFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") name, [Class](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Class.html> "class or interface in java.lang")<? extends [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")> functionClass)

Registers a [`UserDefinedFunction`](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") class as a temporary system function.

Compared to `createTemporaryFunction(String, Class)`, system functions are identified by a global name that is independent of the current catalog and current database. Thus, this method allows to extend the set of built-in system functions like `TRIM`, `ABS`, etc.

Temporary functions can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent function under a given name exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent function available again one can drop the corresponding temporary system function.

Parameters:
    `name` \- The name under which the function will be registered globally.
    `functionClass` \- The function class containing the implementation.
    * ### createTemporarySystemFunction

void createTemporarySystemFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") name, [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") functionInstance)

Registers a [`UserDefinedFunction`](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") instance as a temporary system function.

Compared to `createTemporarySystemFunction(String, Class)`, this method takes a function instance that might have been parameterized before (e.g. through its constructor). This might be useful for more interactive sessions. Make sure that the instance is [`Serializable`](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/io/Serializable.html> "class or interface in java.io").

Compared to `createTemporaryFunction(String, UserDefinedFunction)`, system functions are identified by a global name that is independent of the current catalog and current database. Thus, this method allows to extend the set of built-in system functions like `TRIM`, `ABS`, etc.

Temporary functions can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent function under a given name exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent function available again one can drop the corresponding temporary system function.

Parameters:
    `name` \- The name under which the function will be registered globally.
    `functionInstance` \- The (possibly pre-configured) function instance containing the implementation.
    * ### dropTemporarySystemFunction

boolean dropTemporarySystemFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") name)

Drops a temporary system function registered under the given name.

If a permanent function with the given name exists, it will be used from now on for any queries that reference this name.

Parameters:
    `name` \- The name under which the function has been registered globally.
Returns:
    true if a function existed under the given name and was removed
    * ### createFunction

void createFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [Class](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Class.html> "class or interface in java.lang")<? extends [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")> functionClass)

Registers a [`UserDefinedFunction`](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") class as a catalog function in the given path.

Compared to system functions with a globally defined name, catalog functions are always (implicitly or explicitly) identified by a catalog and database.

There must not be another function (temporary or permanent) registered under the same path.

Parameters:

void createTemporaryFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [Class](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Class.html> "class or interface in java.lang")<? extends [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")> functionClass)

Registers a [`UserDefinedFunction`](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") class as a temporary catalog function.

Compared to `createTemporarySystemFunction(String, Class)` with a globally defined name, catalog functions are always (implicitly or explicitly) identified by a catalog and database.

Temporary functions can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent function under a given name exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent function available again one can drop the corresponding temporary function.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the function will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `functionClass` \- The function class containing the implementation.
    * ### createTemporaryFunction

void createTemporaryFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") functionInstance)

Registers a [`UserDefinedFunction`](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions") instance as a temporary catalog function.

Compared to `createTemporaryFunction(String, Class)`, this method takes a function instance that might have been parameterized before (e.g. through its constructor). This might be useful for more interactive sessions. Make sure that the instance is [`Serializable`](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/io/Serializable.html> "class or interface in java.io").

Compared to `createTemporarySystemFunction(String, UserDefinedFunction)` with a globally defined name, catalog functions are always (implicitly or explicitly) identified by a catalog and database.

Temporary functions can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent function under a given name exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent function available again one can drop the corresponding temporary function.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the function will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `functionInstance` \- The (possibly pre-configured) function instance containing the implementation.
    * ### createTemporaryFunction

boolean dropTemporaryFunction([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path)

Drops a temporary catalog function registered in the given path.

If a permanent function with the given path exists, it will be used from now on for any queries that reference this path.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the function will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
Returns:

    * ### createTemporaryTable

void createTemporaryTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [TableDescriptor](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor)

Registers the given [`TableDescriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a temporary catalog table.

The [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

Examples:

          tEnv.createTemporaryTable("MyTable", TableDescriptor.forConnector("datagen")
             .schema(Schema.newBuilder()
               .column("f0", DataTypes.STRING())
               .build())
             .option(DataGenOptions.ROWS_PER_SECOND, 10)
             .option("fields.f0.kind", "random")
             .build());


Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the table will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    * ### createTemporaryTable

void createTemporaryTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [TableDescriptor](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor, boolean ignoreIfExists)

Registers the given [`TableDescriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a temporary catalog table.

The [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

Examples:

          tEnv.createTemporaryTable("MyTable", TableDescriptor.forConnector("datagen")
             .schema(Schema.newBuilder()
               .column("f0", DataTypes.STRING())
               .build())
             .option(DataGenOptions.ROWS_PER_SECOND, 10)
             .option("fields.f0.kind", "random")
             .build(),
            true);


Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the table will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    `ignoreIfExists` \- If a table exists under the given path and this flag is set, no operation is executed. An exception is thrown otherwise.
    * ### createTable

void createTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [TableDescriptor](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor)

Registers the given [`TableDescriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a catalog table.

The [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

If the table should not be permanently stored in a catalog, use `createTemporaryTable(String, TableDescriptor)` instead.

Examples:

          tEnv.createTable("MyTable", TableDescriptor.forConnector("datagen")
             .schema(Schema.newBuilder()
               .column("f0", DataTypes.STRING())
               .build())
             .option(DataGenOptions.ROWS_PER_SECOND, 10)
             .option("fields.f0.kind", "random")
             .build());


Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the table will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    * ### createTable

boolean createTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [TableDescriptor](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor, boolean ignoreIfExists)

Registers the given [`TableDescriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a catalog table.

The [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

If the table should not be permanently stored in a catalog, use `createTemporaryTable(String, TableDescriptor, boolean)` instead.

Examples:

          tEnv.createTable("MyTable", TableDescriptor.forConnector("datagen")
             .schema(Schema.newBuilder()
               .column("f0", DataTypes.STRING())
               .build())
             .option(DataGenOptions.ROWS_PER_SECOND, 10)
             .option("fields.f0.kind", "random")
             .build(),
            true);


Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the table will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogTable`](<../catalog/CatalogTable.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    `ignoreIfExists` \- If a table exists under the given path and this flag is set, no operation is executed. An exception is thrown otherwise.
Returns:
    true if table was created in the given path, false if a permanent object already exists in the given path.
    * ### registerTable

[@Deprecated](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Deprecated.html> "class or interface in java.lang") void registerTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") name, [Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") table)

Deprecated.

use `createTemporaryView(String, Table)`

Registers a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") under a unique name in the TableEnvironment's catalog. Registered tables can be referenced in SQL queries.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

Parameters:
    `name` \- The name under which the table will be registered.
    `table` \- The table to register.

Registers a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") API object as a temporary view similar to SQL temporary views.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

    `ignoreIfExists` \- If a model exists and the given flag is set, no operation is executed. An exception is thrown otherwise.
    * ### createTemporaryModel

void createTemporaryModel([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [ModelDescriptor](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor)

Registers the given [`ModelDescriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a temporary catalog model similar to SQL models.

The [`descriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogModel`](<../catalog/CatalogModel.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the model will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogModel`](<../catalog/CatalogModel.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    * ### createTemporaryModel

void createTemporaryModel([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [ModelDescriptor](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor, boolean ignoreIfExists)

Registers the given [`ModelDescriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") as a temporary catalog model similar to SQL models.

The [`descriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") is converted into a [`CatalogModel`](<../catalog/CatalogModel.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") and stored in the catalog.

Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a permanent object in a given path exists, it will be inaccessible in the current session. To make the permanent object available again one can drop the corresponding temporary object.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path under which the model will be registered. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `descriptor` \- Template for creating a [`CatalogModel`](<../catalog/CatalogModel.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog") instance.
    `ignoreIfExists` \- If a model exists and the given flag is set, no operation is executed. An exception is thrown otherwise.
    * ### scan

[@Deprecated](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Deprecated.html> "class or interface in java.lang") [Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") scan([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang")... tablePath)

Deprecated.

use `from(String)`

Scans a registered table and returns the resulting [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").

A table to scan must be registered in the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api"). It can be either directly registered or be an external member of a [`Catalog`](<../catalog/Catalog.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.catalog").

See the documentation of `useDatabase(String)` or `useCatalog(String)` for the rules on the path resolution.

Examples:

Scanning a directly registered table.

          Table tab = tableEnv.scan("tableName");


Scanning a table from a registered catalog.

          Table tab = tableEnv.scan("catalogName", "dbName", "tableName");


Parameters:
    `tablePath` \- The path of the table to scan.
Returns:
    The resulting [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").
See Also:

      * `useCatalog(String)`
      * `useDatabase(String)`
    * ### from

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") from([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path)

Reads a registered table and returns the resulting [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").

A table to scan must be registered in the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").

See the documentation of `useDatabase(String)` or `useCatalog(String)` for the rules on the path resolution.

Examples:

Reading a table from default catalog and database.

          Table tab = tableEnv.from("tableName");


Reading a table from a registered catalog.

          Table tab = tableEnv.from("catalogName.dbName.tableName");


Reading a table from a registered catalog with escaping. Dots in e.g. a database name must be escaped.

          Table tab = tableEnv.from("catalogName.`db.Name`.Table");


Note that the returned [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") is an API object and only contains a pipeline description. It actually corresponds to a _view_ in SQL terms. Call [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>) to trigger an execution.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path of a table API object to scan.
Returns:
    The [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.
See Also:

      * `useCatalog(String)`
      * `useDatabase(String)`
    * ### from

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") from([TableDescriptor](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor)

Returns a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") backed by the given [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api").

The [`descriptor`](<TableDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") won't be registered in the catalog, but it will be propagated directly in the operation tree. Note that calling this method multiple times, even with the same descriptor, results in multiple temporary tables. In such cases, it is recommended to register it under a name using `createTemporaryTable(String, TableDescriptor)` and reference it via `from(String)`.

Examples:

          Table table = tEnv.from(TableDescriptor.forConnector("datagen")
             .schema(Schema.newBuilder()
               .column("f0", DataTypes.STRING())
               .build())
             .build());


Note that the returned [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") is an API object and only contains a pipeline description. It actually corresponds to a _view_ in SQL terms. Call [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>) to trigger an execution.

Returns:
    The [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.
    * ### fromCall

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromCall([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, [Object](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Object.html> "class or interface in java.lang")... arguments)

Returns a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") backed by a call to a process table function (PTF).

A PTF maps zero, one, or multiple tables to a new table. PTFs are the most powerful function kind for Flink SQL and Table API. They enable implementing user-defined operators that can be as feature-rich as built-in operations. PTFs have access to Flink's managed state, event-time and timer services, underlying table changelogs, and can take multiple partitioned tables to produce a new table.

This method assumes a call to a previously registered function.

Example:

          env.createFunction("MyPTF", MyPTF.class);

           Table table = env.fromCall(
             "MyPTF",
             table.partitionBy($("key")).asArgument("input_table"),
             lit("Bob").asArgument("default_name"),
             lit(42).asArgument("default_threshold")
           );


A PTF can digest tables either per row (with row semantics) or per set (with set semantics). For set semantics ([`ArgumentTrait.SET_SEMANTIC_TABLE`](<../annotation/ArgumentTrait.html#SET_SEMANTIC_TABLE>)), make sure to partition the table first using [`Table.partitionBy(Expression...)`](<Table.html#partitionBy\(org.apache.flink.table.expressions.Expression...\)>).

Parameters:
    `path` \- The path of a function.
    `arguments` \- Table and scalar argument [`Expressions`](<Expressions.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api").
Returns:
    The [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.
See Also:

      * [`Expressions.call(String, Object...)`](<Expressions.html#call\(java.lang.String,java.lang.Object...\)>)
      * [`ProcessTableFunction`](<../functions/ProcessTableFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")
    * ### fromCall

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromCall([Class](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Class.html> "class or interface in java.lang")<? extends [UserDefinedFunction](<../functions/UserDefinedFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")> function, [Object](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Object.html> "class or interface in java.lang")... arguments)

Returns a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") backed by a call to a process table function (PTF).

A PTF maps zero, one, or multiple tables to a new table. PTFs are the most powerful function kind for Flink SQL and Table API. They enable implementing user-defined operators that can be as feature-rich as built-in operations. PTFs have access to Flink's managed state, event-time and timer services, underlying table changelogs, and can take multiple partitioned tables to produce a new table.

This method assumes a call to an unregistered, inline function.

Example:

          Table table = env.fromCall(
             MyPTF.class,
             table.partitionBy($("key")).asArgument("input_table")
           );


A PTF can digest tables either per row (with row semantics) or per set (with set semantics). For set semantics ([`ArgumentTrait.SET_SEMANTIC_TABLE`](<../annotation/ArgumentTrait.html#SET_SEMANTIC_TABLE>)), make sure to partition the table first using [`Table.partitionBy(Expression...)`](<Table.html#partitionBy\(org.apache.flink.table.expressions.Expression...\)>).

Parameters:
    `function` \- The class containing the function's logic.
    `arguments` \- Table and scalar argument [`Expressions`](<Expressions.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api").
Returns:
    The [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.
See Also:

      * [`Expressions.call(Class, Object...)`](<Expressions.html#call\(java.lang.Class,java.lang.Object...\)>)
      * [`ProcessTableFunction`](<../functions/ProcessTableFunction.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.functions")
    * ### fromModel

[Model](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromModel([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") modelPath)

Returns a [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object that is backed by the specified model path.

This method creates a [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object from a given model path in the catalog. The model path can be fully or partially qualified (e.g., "catalog.db.model" or just "model"), depending on the current catalog and database context.

The returned [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object can be used for further transformations or as input to other operations in the Table API.

Example:

          Model model = tableEnv.fromModel("my_model");


Parameters:
    `modelPath` \- The path of the model in the catalog.
Returns:
    The [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the model resource.
    * ### fromModel

[Model](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") fromModel([ModelDescriptor](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") descriptor)

Returns a [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object that is backed by the specified [`ModelDescriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api").

This method creates a [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object using the provided [`ModelDescriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api"), which contains the necessary information to identify and configure the model resource in the catalog.

The returned [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object can be used for further transformations or as input to other operations in the Table API.

Example:

          ModelDescriptor descriptor = ...;
           Model model = tableEnv.from(descriptor);


Parameters:
    `descriptor` \- The [`ModelDescriptor`](<ModelDescriptor.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") describing the model resource.
Returns:
    The [`Model`](<Model.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object representing the model resource.

    * ### dropTemporaryTable

boolean dropTemporaryTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path)

Drops a temporary table registered in the given path.

If a permanent table with a given path exists, it will be used from now on for any queries that reference this path.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The given path under which the temporary table will be dropped. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
Returns:
    true if a table existed in the given path and was removed
    * ### dropTable

boolean dropTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path)

Drops a table registered in the given path.

This method can only drop permanent objects. Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a temporary object exists in a given path, make sure to drop the temporary object first using `dropTemporaryTable(java.lang.String)`.

Compared to SQL, this method will not throw an error if the table does not exist. Use `dropTable(java.lang.String, boolean)` to change the default behavior.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The given path under which the table will be dropped. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
Returns:
    true if table existed in the given path and was dropped, false if table didn't exist in the given path.
    * ### dropTable

boolean dropTable([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path, boolean ignoreIfNotExists)

Drops a table registered in the given path.

This method can only drop permanent objects. Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a temporary object exists in a given path, make sure to drop the temporary object first using `dropTemporaryTable(java.lang.String)`.

Parameters:
    `path` \- The given path under which the given table will be dropped. See also the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") class description for the format of the path.
    `ignoreIfNotExists` \- If false exception will be thrown if the view to drop does not exist.
Returns:
    true if table existed in the given path and was dropped, false if table didn't exist in the given path.
    * ### dropTemporaryView

boolean dropTemporaryView([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") path)

If a permanent table or view with a given path exists, it will be used from now on for any queries that reference this path.

    true if a view existed in the given path and was removed
    * ### dropView

This method can only drop permanent objects. Temporary objects can shadow permanent ones. If a temporary object exists in a given path, make sure to drop the temporary object first using `dropTemporaryView(java.lang.String)`.

Compared to SQL, this method will not throw an error if the view does not exist. Use `dropView(java.lang.String, boolean)` to change the default behavior.

    * ### dropView

default [String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") explainSql([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") statement, [ExplainDetail](<ExplainDetail.html> "enum class in org.apache.flink.table.api")... extraDetails)

[String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") explainSql([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") statement, [ExplainFormat](<ExplainFormat.html> "enum class in org.apache.flink.table.api") format, [ExplainDetail](<ExplainDetail.html> "enum class in org.apache.flink.table.api")... extraDetails)

    AST and the execution plan.
    * ### getCompletionHints

[@Deprecated](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/Deprecated.html> "class or interface in java.lang") [String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang")[] getCompletionHints([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") statement, int position)

Deprecated.

Will be removed in the next release

Returns completion hints for the given statement at the given cursor position. The completion happens case insensitively.

Parameters:
    `statement` \- Partial or slightly incorrect SQL statement
    `position` \- cursor position
Returns:
    completion hints that fit at the current cursor position
    * ### sqlQuery

[Table](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") sqlQuery([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") query)

Evaluates a SQL query on registered tables and returns a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.

All tables and other objects referenced by the query must be registered in the [`TableEnvironment`](<TableEnvironment.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api"). For example, use `createTemporaryView(String, Table)`) for referencing a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object or `createTemporarySystemFunction(String, Class)` for functions.

Alternatively, a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object is automatically registered when its `Table#toString()` method is called, for example when it is embedded into a string. Hence, SQL queries can directly reference a [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object inline (i.e. anonymous) as follows:

          Table table = ...;
           String tableName = table.toString();
           // the table is not registered to the table environment
           tEnv.sqlQuery("SELECT * FROM " + tableName + " WHERE a > 12");


Note that the returned [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") is an API object and only contains a pipeline description. It actually corresponds to a _view_ in SQL terms. Call [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>) to trigger an execution or use `executeSql(String)` directly.

Parameters:
    `query` \- The SQL query to evaluate.
Returns:
    The [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") object describing the pipeline for further transformations.
    * ### executeSql

[TableResult](<TableResult.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") executeSql([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") statement)

Executes the given single statement and returns the execution result.

The statement can be DDL/DML/DQL/SHOW/DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN/USE. For DML and DQL, this method returns [`TableResult`](<TableResult.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") once the job has been submitted. For DDL and DCL statements, [`TableResult`](<TableResult.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") is returned once the operation has finished.

If multiple pipelines should insert data into one or more sink tables as part of a single execution, use a [`StatementSet`](<StatementSet.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") (see `createStatementSet()`).

By default, all DML operations are executed asynchronously. Use [`TableResult.await()`](<TableResult.html#await\(\)>) or [`TableResult.getJobClient()`](<TableResult.html#getJobClient\(\)>) to monitor the execution. Set [`TableConfigOptions.TABLE_DML_SYNC`](<config/TableConfigOptions.html#TABLE_DML_SYNC>) for always synchronous execution.

Returns:
    content for DQL/SHOW/DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN, the affected row count for `DML` (-1 means unknown), or a string message ("OK") for other statements.
    * ### getCurrentCatalog

[String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") getCurrentCatalog()

Gets the current default catalog name of the current session.

Returns:
    The current default catalog name that is used for the path resolution.
See Also:

      * `useCatalog(String)`
    * ### useCatalog

void useCatalog(@Nullable [String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") catalogName)

Sets the current catalog to the given value. It also sets the default database to the catalog's default one. See also `useDatabase(String)`.

This is used during the resolution of object paths. Both the catalog and database are optional when referencing catalog objects such as tables, views etc. The algorithm looks for requested objects in following paths in that order:

      1. `[current-catalog].[current-database].[requested-path]`
      2. `[current-catalog].[requested-path]`
      3. `[requested-path]`

Example:

Given structure with default catalog set to `default_catalog` and default database set to `default_database`.

    root:
       |- default_catalog
           |- default_database
               |- tab1
           |- db1
               |- tab1
       |- cat1
           |- db1
               |- tab1


The following table describes resolved paths:

Requested path | Resolved path
---|---
tab1 | default_catalog.default_database.tab1
db1.tab1 | default_catalog.db1.tab1
cat1.db1.tab1 | cat1.db1.tab1

You can unset the current catalog by passing a null value. If the current catalog is unset, you need to use fully qualified identifiers.

Parameters:
    `catalogName` \- The name of the catalog to set as the current default catalog.
See Also:

      * `useDatabase(String)`
    * ### getCurrentDatabase

[String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") getCurrentDatabase()

Gets the current default database name of the running session.

Returns:
    The name of the current database of the current catalog.
See Also:

      * `useDatabase(String)`
    * ### useDatabase

void useDatabase(@Nullable [String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") databaseName)

Sets the current default database. It has to exist in the current catalog. That path will be used as the default one when looking for unqualified object names.

This is used during the resolution of object paths. Both the catalog and database are optional when referencing catalog objects such as tables, views etc. The algorithm looks for requested objects in following paths in that order:

      1. `[current-catalog].[current-database].[requested-path]`
      2. `[current-catalog].[requested-path]`
      3. `[requested-path]`

Example:

Given structure with default catalog set to `default_catalog` and default database set to `default_database`.

    root:
       |- default_catalog
           |- default_database
               |- tab1
           |- db1
               |- tab1
       |- cat1
           |- db1
               |- tab1


The following table describes resolved paths:

Requested path | Resolved path
---|---
tab1 | default_catalog.default_database.tab1
db1.tab1 | default_catalog.db1.tab1
cat1.db1.tab1 | cat1.db1.tab1

You can unset the current database by passing a null value. If the current database is unset, you need to qualify identifiers at least with the database name.

Parameters:
    `databaseName` \- The name of the database to set as the current database.
See Also:

      * `useCatalog(String)`
    * ### getConfig

[TableConfig](<TableConfig.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") getConfig()

Returns the table config that defines the runtime behavior of the Table API.

    * ### createStatementSet

[StatementSet](<StatementSet.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") createStatementSet()

Returns a [`StatementSet`](<StatementSet.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") that accepts pipelines defined by DML statements or [`Table`](<Table.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") objects. The planner can optimize all added statements together and then submit them as one job.

    * ### loadPlan

[@Experimental](<../../annotation/Experimental.html> "annotation interface in org.apache.flink.annotation") [CompiledPlan](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") loadPlan([PlanReference](<PlanReference.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") planReference) throws [TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")

Loads a plan from a [`PlanReference`](<PlanReference.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") into a [`CompiledPlan`](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").

Compiled plans can be persisted and reloaded across Flink versions. They describe static pipelines to ensure backwards compatibility and enable stateful streaming job upgrades. See [`CompiledPlan`](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") and the website documentation for more information.

This method will parse the input reference and will validate the plan. The returned instance can be executed via [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>).

Note: The compiled plan feature is not supported in batch mode.

Throws:
    `[TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")` \- if the plan cannot be loaded from the filesystem, or from classpath resources, or if the plan is invalid.
    * ### compilePlanSql

[@Experimental](<../../annotation/Experimental.html> "annotation interface in org.apache.flink.annotation") [CompiledPlan](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") compilePlanSql([String](<https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/java/lang/String.html> "class or interface in java.lang") stmt) throws [TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")

Compiles a SQL DML statement into a [`CompiledPlan`](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api").

Compiled plans can be persisted and reloaded across Flink versions. They describe static pipelines to ensure backwards compatibility and enable stateful streaming job upgrades. See [`CompiledPlan`](<CompiledPlan.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") and the website documentation for more information.

Note: Only `INSERT INTO` is supported at the moment.

Note: The compiled plan feature is not supported in batch mode.

Throws:
    `[TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")` \- if the SQL statement is invalid or if the plan cannot be persisted.
See Also:

      * [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>)
      * `loadPlan(PlanReference)`
    * ### executePlan

[@Experimental](<../../annotation/Experimental.html> "annotation interface in org.apache.flink.annotation") default [TableResult](<TableResult.html> "interface in org.apache.flink.table.api") executePlan([PlanReference](<PlanReference.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api") planReference) throws [TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")

Shorthand for `tEnv.loadPlan(planReference).execute()`.

Throws:
    `[TableException](<TableException.html> "class in org.apache.flink.table.api")`
See Also:

      * `loadPlan(PlanReference)`
      * [`Executable.execute()`](<Executable.html#execute\(\)>)
